title: Supply Chain Attack
description: Compromising software or hardware in the supply chain to attack downstream users at scale.
fullExplanation: Supply chain attacks target the less-secure elements in the software/hardware supply chain rather than attacking the final target directly. By compromising a trusted vendor, library, or build system, attackers can reach thousands of organizations simultaneously. The SolarWinds attack (2020) compromised the build pipeline of a network monitoring tool used by 18,000 organizations including US government agencies.

notableExamples.name: SolarWinds (2020)
notableExamples.description: Compromised Orion build pipeline — malicious DLL in signed update — 18,000 victims including US Treasury, DoD

notableExamples.name: XZ Utils (2024)
notableExamples.description: 2-year social engineering campaign to backdoor SSH via liblzma in Linux distros

notableExamples.name: event-stream (2018)
notableExamples.description: npm package maintainer transferred ownership — malicious code added targeting Bitcoin wallets

notableExamples.name: Kaseya VSA (2021)
notableExamples.description: REvil exploited MSP software — ransomware deployed to 1,500 businesses via trusted management tool

notableExamples.name: CCleaner (2017)
notableExamples.description: Legitimate software update contained backdoor — 2.27 million downloads

attackVectors.description: Inject malicious code into CI/CD pipeline — signed legitimate binary contains backdoor
Upload malicious package with same name as internal package to public registry
Publish malicious package with similar name (reqeusts vs requests)
Social engineer or compromise legitimate package maintainer
Insert malicious chip during manufacturing (Bloomberg Supermicro allegations)

keyFacts: SolarWinds: attackers had access for 9 months before discovery
npm has 2M+ packages — impossible to audit all dependencies
Average enterprise uses 500+ open source components
SBOM (Software Bill of Materials): inventory of all components — US Executive Order 14028 mandates it
Sigstore: cryptographically sign and verify software artifacts
Dependency confusion: internal package names leaked via error messages
Left-pad incident (2016): 11-line package removal broke thousands of builds

defenses.name: SBOM
defenses.description: Software Bill of Materials — know every component and version in your software

defenses.name: Dependency Pinning
defenses.description: Pin exact versions and verify checksums — prevent unexpected updates

defenses.name: Code Signing
defenses.description: Verify signatures on all software updates and packages

defenses.name: Private Package Registry
defenses.description: Mirror dependencies internally — prevent dependency confusion

defenses.name: Vendor Security Assessment
defenses.description: Audit security practices of critical software vendors

defenses.name: Network Monitoring
defenses.description: Detect unusual outbound connections from trusted software
